title: Auditing household single-use packaging
subtitle: Measuring what packaging you buy by category and identifying the 2 or 3 substitutions with the largest impact
excerpt: The average UK household produces around 500 kg of municipal waste per year, of which roughly 30 percent is packaging. A two-week packaging audit by product category identifies which items drive most of the packaging weight and cost, and where substitutions are feasible. Most households find that 3 to 5 product categories account for over 60 percent of their packaging volume.
sourceNotes: WRAP UK Packaging Flows 2022 data. DEFRA UK waste statistics 2021. Which? Guide to reducing packaging waste at home.

glossaryTerms.definition: An economic model that prioritises keeping products and materials in use for as long as possible through reuse, repair, and sharing, rather than a linear take-make-dispose pattern.
The household recycling collection service run by local authorities, collecting separated materials (card, glass, plastic, metal) at the kerbside on a scheduled basis.

content.text: WRAP data shows that UK households purchase an average of 25 kg of plastic packaging per person per year. Of that, 50 to 55 percent is recycled via

or supermarket collection points. The remainder goes to landfill or incineration. The packaging audit identifies where your household sits relative to those averages and which substitutions are available to you.

For two weeks, stack all packaging separately before recycling or binning. Group it in four piles: plastic film and soft plastics, hard plastic containers (bottles, tubs, pots, trays), glass and metal, and card and paper.

At the end of the two weeks, count the items in each pile and estimate the volume (a standard shopping bag holds roughly 5 litres; a bin bag holds 40 litres). You are looking at relative volume, not exact weights.

Within the largest pile, identify the top 5 to 10 products by count. For most households this is: soft plastic bags from bread and vegetables, plastic film from meat trays, plastic pots from yoghurt and cream, hard trays from ready meals, and cling film.

Target the top 2 or 3 items by volume rather than switching everything at once. A household that moves five high-volume items to zero-packaging alternatives reduces plastic waste more than one that switches every minor item. High-impact substitutions include loose vegetables instead of pre-packed bags, a 1 kg block of cheddar instead of multiple smaller bags, and dried pasta in a paper bag. Loose vegetables are also 30 to 50 percent cheaper per kg than pre-packed. Bar soap and shampoo bars replace pump bottles.

Most local authorities recycle rigid plastic bottles and pots, glass, metal cans, and cardboard at the kerbside. Soft plastic film (bread bags, vegetable bags, frozen food bags, biscuit wrappers) is not accepted at the kerbside in most areas but can be returned to large supermarkets via the Flexible Plastic Fund recycling points. The Recycling Locator at recyclenow.com shows what is accepted at your postcode. Do not mix non-recyclable plastic in the recycling bin: contaminated loads are rejected, and the whole bag goes to landfill.

A packaging audit measures what you buy, not the upstream packaging in food production. Upstream factory packaging is roughly 5 to 10 times the household packaging by weight. The audit is useful for household-level changes. The larger impact is in food system decisions beyond the scope of a domestic audit. This is not an argument against auditing. The
works at every scale. Household-level changes across 27 million UK households add up.

Loose produce is usually cheaper per kg than pre-packed, and buying a larger format (1 kg yoghurt instead of four 250 g pots) reduces cost per unit. The expensive substitutes are typically niche zero-waste brands: stick to commodity products in low-packaging formats rather than premium packaging-free alternatives.

attrs.symptom: Supermarket flexible plastic return points are always full

content.text: This is a sign of local uptake, which is positive. Try a different day or a different store. Store soft plastics in a bag at home until the collection point has capacity; the delay does not affect the recyclability.
